title: Find minimum element in array
description: Given an array of integers return the minimum element

examples.explanation:
Array: [5, 3, 8, 1, 2]
Step 1: Check element 5 at index 0
  Compare 5 with 3: 5 > 3 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 2: Check element 3 at index 1
  Compare 3 with 5: 3 < 5 → continue
  Compare 3 with 8: 3 < 8 → continue
  Compare 3 with 1: 3 > 1 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 3: Check element 8 at index 2
  Compare 8 with 5: 8 > 5 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 4: Check element 1 at index 3
  Compare 1 with 5: 1 < 5 → continue
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 → continue
  Compare 1 with 8: 1 < 8 → continue
  Compare 1 with 2: 1 < 2 → continue
  Finished all comparisons without finding smaller element
  Return 1

Array: [-2, -5, -1, -8, -3]
Step 1: Check -2 at index 0
  Compare -2 with -5: -2 > -5 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 2: Check -5 at index 1
  Compare -5 with -2: -5 < -2 → continue
  Compare -5 with -1: -5 < -1 → continue
  Compare -5 with -8: -5 > -8 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 3: Check -1 at index 2
  Compare -1 with -2: -1 > -2 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 4: Check -8 at index 3
  Compare -8 with -2: -8 < -2 → continue
  Compare -8 with -5: -8 < -5 → continue
  Compare -8 with -1: -8 < -1 → continue
  Compare -8 with -3: -8 < -3 → continue
  Finished all comparisons → Return -8

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare every element with every other element to find the minimum. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Compare every element with every other element to find the minimum.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach to finding the minimum element in an array works by comparing every element with every other element to determine if it's the smallest. The intuition is simple: for an element to be the minimum, it must be less than or equal to all other elements in the array. We can verify this by checking each element against all others. For each element at index i, we compare it with every element at index j (where j ≠ i). If element i is less than or equal to all other elements, we've found our minimum. The algorithm starts by assuming the first element is the minimum, then for each subsequent element, we compare it with all other elements. If we find any element that's smaller than our current candidate, we update our candidate
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 3, 8, 1, 2]
Step 1: Check element 5 at index 0
  Compare 5 with 3: 5 > 3 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 2: Check element 3 at index 1
  Compare 3 with 5: 3 < 5 → continue
  Compare 3 with 8: 3 < 8 → continue
  Compare 3 with 1: 3 > 1 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 3: Check element 8 at index 2
  Compare 8 with 5: 8 > 5 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 4: Check element 1 at index 3
  Compare 1 with 5: 1 < 5 → continue
  Compare 1 with 3: 1 < 3 → continue
  Compare 1 with 8: 1 < 8 → continue
  Compare 1 with 2: 1 < 2
... (trace simplified)

Array: [-2, -5, -1, -8, -3]
Step 1: Check -2 at index 0
  Compare -2 with -5: -2 > -5 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 2: Check -5 at index 1
  Compare -5 with -2: -5 < -2 → continue
  Compare -5 with -1: -5 < -1 → continue
  Compare -5 with -8: -5 > -8 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 3: Check -1 at index 2
  Compare -1 with -2: -1 > -2 → not minimum
  Break inner loop
Step 4: Check -8 at index 3
  Compare -8 with -2: -8 < -2 → continue
  Compare -8 with -5: -8 < -5 → continue
  Compare -8 with -1: -8 < -1 → continue
  Compare -8 wit
... (trace simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Is Element the Minimum?
ladders.desc: Check if a given element is smaller than or equal to all others.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach. Given an array and a target index, we need to determine whether the element at that index is the minimum element in the entire array. The intuition is that for an element to be the minimum, it must be less than or equal to every other element in the array. We verify this by comparing our target element with each other element one by one. If we find any element that's strictly smaller than our target, we immediately know our target isn't the minimum. Only if we complete all comparisons without finding a smaller element can we confirm our target is indeed the minimum. This connects to L0 as the repeated application of this check for each element in the array. The time complexity for
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 3, 8, 1, 2], Check if element at index 3 (value 1) is minimum
Step 1: Compare with index 0 (value 5): 1 < 5 → continue
Step 2: Compare with index 1 (value 3): 1 < 3 → continue
Step 3: Compare with index 2 (value 8): 1 < 8 → continue
Step 4: Compare with index 4 (value 2): 1 < 2 → continue
Step 5: Finished all comparisons without finding smaller element
Return True

Array: [4, 2, 7, 2, 5], Check if element at index 0 (value 4) is minimum
Step 1: Compare with index 1 (value 2): 4 > 2 → found smaller element
Return False immediately

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two elements to determine their relative ordering
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental operation at the heart of the brute force approach. We need to compare two elements to determine which is smaller (or if they're equal). The intuition is simple: given two numbers a and b, we can determine their relationship using comparison operators. If a < b, then a is smaller. If a > b, then b is smaller. If a == b, they're equal. In the context of finding minimums, we typically care about the 'less than' relationship. This connects to L1 as the basic building block used repeatedly in the element comparison checker. The time complexity is O(1) - a single comparison operation. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes: 1) Confusing comparison operators (<, >, <=, >=), 2) Not considering equal elements properly, 3) Trying to compare non-numeric types, 4) Off-by-one errors when comparing indices rather than values. The real pattern is simply using comparison operators between two values.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: two elements a and b
2. Compare a and b using appropriate operator
3. Return result based on comparison

For minimum checking:
If a > b: return 'b is smaller'
If a <= b: return 'a is not larger than b'

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare 5 and 3
Step 2: 5 > 3 is True
Step 3: Therefore 3 is smaller than 5

Step 1: Compare -2 and -5
Step 2: -2 > -5 is True (because -2 is greater than -5)
Step 3: Therefore -5 is smaller than -2

ladders.title: Building Block — Nested Loop Traversal
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array while tracking current position/index
ladders.explanation:
Array traversal is essential for accessing each element in the brute force approach. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access them using indices (positions). We need to visit each element exactly once in the outer loop of our algorithm. This involves starting at index 0 (the first element), then moving to index 1, then 2, and so on until we reach the last element at index n-1. The connection to L2 is that once we can access elements by index, we can compare them pairwise. Time complexity for traversing an array of size n is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if we only use an index variable. Common mistakes: 1) Starting index at 1 instead of 0, 2) Using '<=' instead of '<' for loop condition (causing index out of bounds), 3) Modifying the array while traversing it, 4) Not handling empty arrays. The real pattern involves a simple for-loop or while-loop with an index counter.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Get array length n
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
   a. Access element at arr[i]
   b. Process element (in our case, check if it's minimum)
3. End when i reaches n

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Array length: 3
Step 1: i = 0, access arr[0] = 5
Step 2: i = 1, access arr[1] = 3
Step 3: i = 2, access arr[2] = 8
Step 4: i = 3, loop ends (3 not < 3)

Array length: 2
Step 1: i = 0, access arr[0] = 10
Step 2: i = 1, access arr[1] = 20
Step 3: i = 2, loop ends (2 not < 2)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing & Access
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index/position
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental operation needed for the brute force approach. Arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. To access an element, we use the array name followed by the index in square brackets: arr[i]. The intuition is that the computer calculates the memory address as: base_address + (index × element_size). This allows constant-time O(1) access to any element if we know its index. This connects to L3 as the operation performed during array traversal. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the access itself. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (first element at index 1), 2) Accessing out of bounds (index < 0 or index >= array length), 3) Confusing value with index, 4) Not understanding that arrays are zero-indexed. The real pattern is simply using the bracket notation with a valid index.

Step-by-step:
1. Have array arr and index i
2. Ensure i is within bounds: 0 <= i < len(arr)
3. Access element using arr[i]
4. Use the retrieved value

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has length 3, index 1 is valid (0 <= 1 < 3)
Step 2: Access arr[1]
Step 3: The element at position 1 is 3
Result: 3

Step 1: Array length 3, index 0 is valid (0 <= 0 < 3)
Step 2: Access arr[0]
Step 3: The element at position 0 is 10
Result: 10

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays as Indexed Sequences
ladders.desc: Arrays as indexed sequences of elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is an ordered collection where each element is stored at a specific index (starting from 0). This allows systematic access and comparison of elements by position — essential for brute force.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty array: []
Step 2: Add first number 10 → [10]
Step 3: Add second number 20 → [10, 20]
Step 4: Add third number 30 → [10, 20, 30]
Step 5: Result is array with 3 elements
Positions: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30

examples.input: Array: [5, 2, 7], identify elements at each position
examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has 3 elements
Step 2: First position (index 0) contains 5
Step 3: Second position (index 1) contains 2
Step 4: Third position (index 2) contains 7
Step 5: Total length = 3

approaches.name: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array once while keeping track of the smallest element seen so far. This is the standard optimal solution for unsorted arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Scan O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the minimum element in an array using a single pass linear scan.
ladders.explanation: The linear scan approach solves the problem by examining each element exactly once while maintaining a running minimum. The intuition is simple: if we want to find the smallest element, we need to check every element at least once since the array is unsorted. We start by assuming the first element is the minimum, then for each subsequent element, we compare it with our current minimum. If we find a smaller element, we update our minimum. This works because we're systematically checking every candidate and keeping the best one found so far. The algorithm: 1. If array is empty, return appropriate value (null/exception). 2. Initialize min_val = arr[0]. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: if arr[i] < min_val, update min_val = arr[i]. 4. Return min_val. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by being the complete application of the key comparison subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty arrays, off-by-one errors in loop bounds, initializing min_val incorrectly (e.g., with 0 instead of first element). Real code pattern: def find_min(arr): if not arr: return None; min_val = arr[0]; for num in arr[1:]: if num < min_val: min_val = num; return min_val.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 3. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=1, 1 < 3 → min_val = 1. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=4, 4 < 1? No. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=1, 1 < 1? No. Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=5, 5 < 1? No. Step 6: i=5, arr[5]=9, 9 < 1? No. Final min_val = 1.

Step 1: min_val = arr[0] = -5. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=-2, -2 < -5? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=-8, -8 < -5 → min_val = -8. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=-1, -1 < -8? No. Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=-3, -3 < -8? No. Final min_val = -8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update Running Minimum
ladders.desc: Implement the core comparison logic that updates the minimum value during array traversal.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the linear scan approach. The intuition is that we need a mechanism to efficiently track the smallest element encountered so far as we iterate. At each step, we compare the current element with our current minimum, and if it's smaller, we update the minimum. This works because it's a greedy approach: we always keep the best candidate found up to that point. The algorithm: 1. Accept current min_val and next element as input. 2. Compare next element with min_val. 3. If next element < min_val, return next element as new min_val; otherwise return current min_val. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the repeated operation in the main loop, and to L2 as it implements the comparison logic. Common mistakes: using <= instead of < (works but less efficient), not handling equal values correctly. Real code pattern: def update_min(current_min, candidate): return candidate if candidate < current_min else current_min.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare candidate (3) with current_min (5). Step 2: 3 < 5 is true. Step 3: Return candidate 3 as new minimum.
Step 1: Compare candidate (7) with current_min (2). Step 2: 7 < 2 is false. Step 3: Return current_min 2 unchanged.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Running Minimum
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the comparison operation that determines if one element is smaller than another.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental comparison operation needed to find minima. The intuition is that to find the smallest element, we need to be able to compare any two elements and determine which is smaller. This is a basic relational operation that underlies many algorithms. The algorithm: given two numbers a and b, return true if a < b, false otherwise. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L1 as it's the predicate used in the update subroutine, and to L3 as it relies on understanding numerical order. Common mistakes: confusing < with >, not considering negative numbers correctly. Real code pattern: def is_smaller(a, b): return a < b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate 3 < 5. Step 2: Since 3 is less than 5, return true.
Step 1: Evaluate 7 < 2. Step 2: Since 7 is not less than 2, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse an array sequentially to access each element exactly once.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to systematically visit each element in an array, which is essential for the linear scan. The intuition is that to examine all elements, we need a controlled way to move from one element to the next. This is typically done with a loop that uses an index variable. The algorithm: 1. Initialize index i = 0. 2. While i < length of array: access arr[i], then increment i by 1. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the index variable. This connects to L2 as it provides the elements to compare, and to L4 as it uses basic indexing operations. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., i <= length), modifying the index incorrectly. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): element = arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, access arr[0]=10. Step 2: i=1, access arr[1]=20. Step 3: i=2, access arr[2]=30. Step 4: i=3, stop (3 not < 3).
Step 1: i=0, access arr[0]=5. Step 2: i=1, stop (1 not < 1).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Using < Operator
ladders.desc: Learn to retrieve an element from an array using its position (index).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves getting the value stored at a specific position in an array. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access any element directly if we know its index (position). Indexing typically starts at 0. The algorithm: given array arr and index i, return arr[i]. Time and space complexity are O(1) for direct access. This connects to L3 as it's how we get elements during iteration, and to L5 as it assumes understanding of arrays. Common mistakes: using invalid indices (negative or beyond array bounds), confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Check index 1 is valid (0 ≤ 1 < 3). Step 2: Return element at position 1: arr[1] = 20.
Step 1: Check index 0 is valid (0 ≤ 0 < 4). Step 2: Return element at position 0: arr[0] = 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integers and Their Order
ladders.explanation: Integers have a natural order on the number line. This ordering allows us to compare any two numbers and determine which is smaller. This is the foundation for all minimum-finding algorithms.

examples.output: Array has 3 elements: 3 at index 0, 7 at index 1, 2 at index 2. Integer 5 is a whole number.
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify array elements: positions 0,1,2 with values 3,7,2. Step 2: Recognize 5 as an integer that could be compared with array elements.

examples.output: Array has 3 elements: -1 at index 0, 0 at index 1, 1 at index 2. Integer -2 is a negative whole number.
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify array elements: positions 0,1,2 with values -1,0,1. Step 2: Recognize -2 as an integer that could be compared with array elements.

approaches.name: Divide & Conquer O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively split the array into halves, find the minimum in each half, and combine results. This demonstrates a classic algorithmic paradigm.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Divide & Conquer O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that splits array into halves to find minimum element.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete implementation of finding the minimum element in an array using the divide and conquer paradigm. The intuition is that by recursively dividing the problem into smaller subproblems, we can solve each subproblem independently and then combine the results. This approach demonstrates how a seemingly simple problem can be solved using a powerful algorithmic technique that scales to more complex problems.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Base case: If the array has only one element, return that element as the minimum.
2. If the array has two elements, compare them and return the smaller one.
3. For larger arrays:
   a. Calculate the midpoint index.
   b. Recursively find the minimum in the left half (from start to midpoint).
   c.
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
find_min(arr, 0, 5):
  mid = 2
  left_min = find_min(arr, 0, 2):
    mid = 1
    left_min = find_min(arr, 0, 1):
      Returns min(5, 3) = 3
    right_min = find_min(arr, 2, 2):
      Returns 8
    Returns min(3, 8) = 3
  right_min = find_min(arr, 3, 5):
    mid = 4
    left_min = find_min(arr, 3, 4):
      Returns min(1, 9) = 1
    right_min = find_min(arr, 5, 5):
      Returns 2
    Returns min(1, 2) = 1
  Returns min(3, 1) = 1

find_min(arr, 0, 4):
  mid = 2
  left_min = find_min(arr, 0, 2):
    mid = 1
    left_min = find_min(arr, 0, 1):
      Returns min(10, 20) = 10
    right_min = find_min(arr, 2, 2):
      Returns 30
    Returns min(10, 30) = 10
  right_min = find_min(arr, 3, 4):
    Returns min(40, 50) = 40
  Returns min(10, 40) = 10

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Min in Segment
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive division logic that splits array into halves.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the recursive splitting mechanism that forms the core of the divide and conquer approach. The intuition is that by repeatedly dividing the problem space in half, we create smaller, more manageable subproblems that can be solved independently. This recursive division is fundamental to many divide and conquer algorithms like merge sort and binary search.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Accept array and indices representing current segment.
2. If segment has 0 or 1 element, return (base case).
3. Calculate midpoint index.
4. Recursively process left half (start to midpoint).
5. Recursively process right half (midpoint+1 to end).
6. Combine results from both halves.

Time complexity: O(log n) for the division pattern its
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace:
split_and_process(arr, 0, 3):
  mid = 1
  Process left half (0-1):
    split_and_process(arr, 0, 1):
      mid = 0
      Process left half (0-0): base case
      Process right half (1-1): base case
      Combine results
  Process right half (2-3):
    split_and_process(arr, 2, 3):
      mid = 2
      Process left half (2-2): base case
      Process right half (3-3): base case
      Combine results
  Combine left and right results

split_and_process(arr, 0, 4):
  mid = 2
  Process left half (0-2):
    split_and_process(arr, 0, 2):
      mid = 1
      Process left half (0-1):
        split_and_process(arr, 0, 1):
          mid = 0
          Process left half (0-0): base case
          Process right half (1-1): base case
          Combine
      Process right half (2-2): base case
      Combine
  Process right half (3-4):
    split_and_process(arr, 3, 4):
      Returns min of two elements
  Combine left and right results

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide & Compare Halves
ladders.desc: The combine step that compares results from divided subproblems.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the core insight of divide and conquer: solving subproblems independently and combining their results. The intuition is that the minimum of an entire array must be the minimum among the minimums of its partitions. This is mathematically sound because if we find the smallest element in the left half and the smallest in the right half, the overall minimum must be the smaller of those two values.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Receive two values representing minimums from left and right subarrays.
2. Compare the two values.
3. Return the smaller value.
4. This simple comparison is the combine operation that merges solutions from subproblems.

Time complexity: O(1) for the comparison operation.
Space complexity: O(1) as it uses constant extra space.

Connection to L1: This comparison logic is what happens at each combine step in the recursive splitting from L1.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to compare and simply returning one value.
2. Using wrong comparison operator (greater than instead of less than).
3. Not handling edge cases like equal values properly.
4. Creating unnecessary complexity instead of simple min() operation.

Real code pattern:
def combine_results(left_min, right_min):
    if left_min < right_min:
        return left_min
    else:
        return right_min

# Or simply:
return min(left_min, right_min)

examples.trace:
1. Receive left_min = 3
2. Receive right_min = 1
3. Compare: 3 < 1? False
4. Compare: 1 < 3? True
5. Return right_min = 1

1. Receive left_min = 5
2. Receive right_min = 8
3. Compare: 5 < 8? True
4. Return left_min = 5

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion on Array Halves
ladders.desc: Recursively divide the array segment into two halves.
ladders.explanation:
This building block implements the divide step of divide-and-conquer. The current array segment is split at the midpoint. The minimum is found recursively in both halves, then the smaller of the two is returned.

Step-by-step:
1. If the segment has only one element, return it (base case).
2. Calculate the midpoint.
3. Recursively solve the left half.
4. Recursively solve the right half.
5. Return the smaller of the two results.

This connects to L2 by providing the recursive structure needed to divide the problem.

examples.trace:
find_min_segment(arr, 0, 3)
  mid = (0+3)//2 = 1
  left_min  = find_min_segment(arr, 0, 1) → returns 3
  right_min = find_min_segment(arr, 2, 3) → returns 1
  return min(3, 1) = 1

find_min_segment(arr, 0, 2)
  mid = (0+2)//2 = 1
  left_min  = find_min_segment(arr, 0, 1) → returns -5
  right_min = find_min_segment(arr, 2, 2) → returns -1
  return min(-5, -1) = -5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Implement function to compare two numbers and return the smaller one.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is fundamental to the divide and conquer approach: comparing two numbers and determining which is smaller. Given two values, return the smaller one (or either if equal).

Step-by-step:
1. Receive two numbers a and b
2. If a < b, return a
3. Else return b

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

This is used when combining minimums from left and right halves.

examples.trace: 5 < 3 is True → return 3
-2 < -1 is True → return -2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays, Indexing & Recursion
ladders.explanation: Arrays allow direct access via indices. Divide-and-Conquer works by recursively breaking the array into smaller identical subproblems (halves) until reaching the trivial base case of a single element.

examples.trace:
Left half: indices 0-1 [5,3]
Right half: indices 2-3 [8,1]
